feedback.text: First I would like to thank the European Commission for all their hard work over the years to protect consumers and consumer privacy. I'm a United States citizen that has spent a considerable amount of my online gaming years in Action Role Playing Games. From the beginning with the original Diablo onward there was always an undercurrent of users, both individual and as time went on groups, that would seek to exploit the game environment any way they could to get an advantage, or even to make money off the development company's IP by developing hacks that they would sell access to. This back and forth battle has played out for 30+ years with the developers never seeming to gain much ground against their digital adversaries. With the advent of much larger games such as Diablo4 and Path of Exile 1/2, that are server authoritative and take considerable investment of time to achieve all the pinnacle milestone of the game and collect the powerful in game items to do so, a large and lucrative illicit business model has arisen to farm items and currency in these games via automated programs referred to as "Bots". These items are then sold to players for real money on sites such as odealo.com, playerup.com, g2g.com, poecurrency.com and many others. This strictly against the ToS of the games, and while no hard numbers are provided by either of the 2 largest developers, there is some evidence that they do ban some users for real money transactions(RMT). The main issue I see with the limited approach of companies like Blizzard and Grinding Gear Games is that they do not do enough to curtail the illicit activities of their players and the organizations that exploit their IP for personal gain. One of the main pathways that allows this, that other games such as Last Epoch and Torchlight Infinite have restricted, is player to player trade. This feature is the primary way a buyer, who most certainly could be a minor due to the age restriction variance for these games in different parts of the world, and a seller are able to connect to effect the IRL monetary transaction via the aforementioned sites. There is also the d2jsp.org site where users can buy "Forum Gold" from the site owner which or possibly even directly from other site users if they "donate" to the site. I believe this donation allows them to access a restricted forum where IRL $$$ transaction can be made to convert the "Forum Gold" to IRL $$$. If the developers remove the ability for players to trade items for real money by removing the direct player to player trading and masking who the seller of an item is by anonymizing the item listing in the game. This would remove a significant amount of the exploitation that occurs in these games. I would also note their was a recent breach at Grinding Gear Games(GGG) where a Steam Admin account belonging to GGG was the victim of a Fishing attack. The breach was used to empty valuable in game items out of many accounts very likely with the intention of converting them to real money via the illicit real money transaction sites. I would recommend including language in the Digital Fairness Act that would require game companies to implement 2 factor authentication, limit player to player trading where such trading is in a server authoritative environment and the items are not account bound or have other strict time limited player to player transfer boundaries. Where possible and advisable due to the adult nature of the game I would also recommend requiring a Real ID style of player identity and age verification. A more stringent identification process would also curtail the practice of selling accounts, some of which are stolen through fishing or malware. Thank you for providing an opportunity for feedback and I look forward to the final outcome of your important work next year.
